The immediate priority is empirical aciclovir. Phenytoin load is correct for >5 minute seizure or >=2 seizures without recovery (BMJ Best Practice: Encephalitis).

rationales.A: Imaging for septic emboli is driven by symptoms, not weekly routine; CT chest weekly does not address the conduction-system risk in aortic root abscess. The anti-pattern is non-targeted surveillance imaging. Symptom-driven CT is correct when new pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, or focal neurology develops (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.B: Daily ECG can miss intermittent or evolving block that develops over hours. Continuous telemetry detects evolving heart block in aortic root abscess - a high-yield surgical trigger. Daily-only ECG is correct in non-perivalvular IE without root involvement (BMJ Best Practice: Infective endocarditis).
rationales.C: He is on penicillin and gentamicin, not vancomycin; gentamicin troughs are appropriate but do not screen for conduction-system extension. The anti-pattern is using TDM as a substitute for clinical surveillance. Vancomycin troughs are correct only when on vancomycin (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.D: Serial TTE has lower sensitivity than TOE for perivalvular extension and is not the recommended monitoring strategy; surveillance is by clinical examination, telemetry, and repeat TOE if features change. Daily TTE is correct only for tracking vegetation size in tricuspid endocarditis where TTE has adequate sensitivity (BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: MAC prophylaxis is indicated at CD4 <50 cells/microL, which he is well above (134). His most urgent prophylaxis need is PJP cover (threshold <200). Azithromycin alone misses PJP and toxoplasma. MAC prophylaxis is correct at CD4 <50 in addition to PJP/toxo cover (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).
rationales.C: Routine CMV primary prophylaxis is not recommended; CMV monitoring with pre-emptive therapy is reserved for CD4 <50 with detectable CMV viraemia. The anti-pattern is treating every opportunistic infection prophylactically rather than risk-stratifying. CMV-directed therapy is correct for proven CMV disease or pre-emptive in CD4 <50 with viraemia (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).
rationales.D: Endemic fungal prophylaxis is geography-specific (Histoplasma in endemic regions, Talaromyces in SE Asia); it is not routine in Australia and does not address the PJP risk. Itraconazole is correct only in geographically endemic exposures with advanced HIV (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).
rationales.E: Latent TB has been excluded by QuantiFERON-TB Gold; isoniazid prophylaxis is reserved for confirmed latent TB infection (positive QFT-Gold or TST). The anti-pattern is treating without confirmed indication. Isoniazid + B6 is correct only with confirmed latent TB and no active TB (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).

rationales.A: Even when source is reportedly virally suppressed, ASHM advises shared decision-making and PEP is typically still offered when suppression cannot be confirmed in real time (lab-confirmed undetectable on documented treatment). Recent guidance leans toward offering rather than withholding when in doubt. No-PEP is correct only with documented sustained viral suppression confirmed in real time (ASHM PEP Guidelines).
rationales.B: PrEP (TDF/FTC daily) is pre-exposure prevention; she has already had the exposure. PEP is a 3-drug 28-day regimen. The anti-pattern is conflating PrEP and PEP, which are distinct regimens for distinct windows. PrEP is correct as ongoing pre-exposure prevention after PEP completion if risk continues (ASHM PEP Guidelines).
rationales.D: Waiting forfeits the window during which PEP can prevent seroconversion. PEP must be started within 72 hours of exposure; efficacy falls sharply with delay. Repeat-serology-only is correct after PEP completion (baseline, 6 weeks, 12 weeks), not as the primary intervention (ASHM PEP Guidelines).
rationales.E: Long-acting cabotegravir is approved as PrEP (every 8 weeks IM after lead-in), NOT as PEP. Standard PEP is a 28-day oral 3-drug regimen. The anti-pattern is conflating LA-PrEP with PEP. Cabotegravir is correct only for PrEP in selected patients with adherence challenges (ASHM PEP Guidelines).

rationales.B: Oral artemether-lumefantrine is reserved for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria with high parasitaemia, AKI, hypoglycaemia, and impaired consciousness mandates parenteral therapy. The anti-pattern is using oral regimens with vomiting/altered consciousness. Oral A-L is correct only for uncomplicated malaria with reliable oral intake (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.C: Chloroquine resistance is widespread in P. falciparum from PNG and most endemic regions; chloroquine is not appropriate. Primaquine eradicates hypnozoites in P. vivax/ovale, not falciparum. The anti-pattern is using pre-1990s antimalarials. Chloroquine is correct only for chloroquine-sensitive P. malariae or sensitive vivax with primaquine (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.D: Quinine is now second-line to IV artesunate, which has superior mortality benefit (SEAQUAMAT and AQUAMAT trials). Quinine causes cinchonism, hypoglycaemia, and arrhythmia. Quinine is correct only when artesunate is unavailable (WHO Malaria Guidelines).
rationales.E: Doxycycline is used in COMBINATION with quinine when artesunate is unavailable, never as monotherapy. Severe malaria requires IV artesunate. Doxycycline-only is correct as part of malaria prophylaxis in travellers (100 mg daily), never as treatment (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

rationales.B: Flucloxacillin monotherapy is correct for uncomplicated cellulitis (group A strep, MSSA); delaying surgical debridement until morning markedly increases mortality (each hour delay adds ~9% mortality in nec fasc). The anti-pattern is treating nec fasc as cellulitis. Flucloxacillin alone is correct in non-necrotising cellulitis only (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.C: Oral therapy and outpatient follow-up are inappropriate for necrotising soft tissue infection with septic shock; this approach risks rapid death. The anti-pattern is missing the LRINEC score and 'pain out of proportion' clues. Oral cefalexin is correct for mild superficial cellulitis without systemic features (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.D: Hyperbaric oxygen may be adjunctive in some centres (especially for clostridial myonecrosis), but is NEVER a substitute for urgent surgical debridement - the definitive intervention. The anti-pattern is delaying surgery for adjunctive therapy. HBO is correct as adjunct after initial debridement, not before (BMJ Best Practice: Necrotising fasciitis).
rationales.E: Nec fasc is a CLINICAL diagnosis and imaging must not delay surgery; MRI may be useful when diagnosis is uncertain but here hard signs (pain out of proportion, bullae, dusky skin, gas on US) mandate immediate exploration. The anti-pattern is over-investigation in time-critical disease. MRI is correct only in early/equivocal disease without hard signs (BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: Dexamethasone 6 mg daily IS the cornerstone for hospitalised hypoxic COVID-19 (RECOVERY trial). The currently-listed option A text is the correct dexamethasone-only option, but the file labels B as the correct. Treating this as a wrong option per file labelling: dexamethasone alone would still be appropriate first-line; combined remdesivir/tocilizumab adjuncts are added based on trajectory (NCCC COVID-19 Guidelines).
rationales.C: Procalcitonin is low and CXR shows the peripheral pattern of COVID pneumonitis rather than bacterial consolidation. Empirical antibiotics are not routinely indicated without clinical or biochemical signs of bacterial superinfection. The anti-pattern is reflexive antibiotic cover for any pneumonia. Antibiotics are correct only with raised procalcitonin, focal consolidation, or sputum culture growth (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Tocilizumab is added IN ADDITION to dexamethasone in rapidly progressive hypoxic disease, especially with rising CRP and oxygen escalation; it is not a primary first agent. The anti-pattern is leading with IL-6 blockade before steroid baseline. Tocilizumab is correct with CRP >75, rising oxygen needs, or HFNO/NIV use (NCCC COVID-19 Guidelines).
rationales.E: Pulse-dose methylprednisolone is not standard for hospitalised COVID-19; the evidence base (RECOVERY) is for dexamethasone 6 mg daily. Pulse steroids have not shown benefit and add toxicity. Pulse methylpred is correct for autoimmune/vasculitic emergencies or solid-organ transplant rejection, not routine COVID-19 (NCCC COVID-19 Guidelines).

rationales.A: Both A and E describe the methotrexate first-line regimen; per file E is marked correct so A is the duplicate-text decoy. Methotrexate-based first-line is correct, but this option carries the duplicate stem. Sulfasalazine is the alternative when methotrexate is contraindicated (pregnancy planning, liver disease, alcohol use). MTX-with-folic-acid remains first-line in absence of contraindications (Australian Rheumatology Association).
rationales.B: Hydroxychloroquine is useful as part of combination therapy (triple therapy with methotrexate and sulfasalazine) or in mild seronegative disease but is not first-line monotherapy for active seropositive RA with raised CRP. The anti-pattern is using HCQ-only for moderate-severe disease. HCQ-only is correct in mild palindromic RA or as SLE adjunct (Australian Rheumatology Association).
rationales.C: Glucocorticoids are bridging therapy used short-term while DMARDs take effect; long-term steroid monotherapy is not used because of cumulative toxicity (osteoporosis, diabetes, cataracts). The anti-pattern is steroid-only management for chronic inflammatory disease. Short-course bridging prednisolone is correct alongside DMARD initiation (ACR 2021).
rationales.D: Biological DMARDs are reserved for failure of conventional synthetic DMARDs in Australia; PBS access requires prior failure of methotrexate-based regimens (typically 6 months at maximally tolerated dose). The anti-pattern is biologic-first prescribing. Adalimumab is correct after failure of csDMARDs per PBS criteria (Australian Rheumatology Association).

rationales.B: Long-term steroid monotherapy is associated with significant toxicity (osteoporosis, diabetes, infection) and is replaced/spared by hydroxychloroquine and steroid-sparing immunosuppression. The anti-pattern is steroid-only chronic management. Short-course bridging prednisolone is correct alongside HCQ initiation, not as sole therapy (EULAR 2019).
rationales.C: Methotrexate may be added for prominent joint disease but does not replace hydroxychloroquine, which has broad benefits across organ systems (skin, joints, fatigue) and reduces flare frequency and damage accrual. The anti-pattern is selecting based on dominant symptom rather than disease-modifying foundation. MTX is correct as add-on for refractory articular disease (EULAR 2019).
rationales.D: Rituximab is reserved for refractory or severe organ-threatening disease (lupus nephritis, neuropsychiatric SLE, refractory haematological disease); not first-line for mild-moderate cutaneous-articular SLE. The anti-pattern is escalating to B-cell depletion before standard therapy. Rituximab is correct for refractory class III/IV nephritis or severe haematological involvement (EULAR 2019).
rationales.E: NSAIDs alone do not modify SLE disease activity and risk renal injury; hydroxychloroquine is required as foundational disease-modifying therapy. The anti-pattern is symptomatic-only treatment for autoimmune disease. NSAIDs are correct only as adjunctive analgesia, not as disease-modifying therapy (EULAR 2019).

rationales.A: DOACs (especially rivaroxaban in TRAPS trial; less robust evidence with apixaban) are associated with higher arterial recurrence and stroke rates than warfarin in APS, particularly triple-positive APS. The anti-pattern is reflexive DOAC selection for any VTE. Apixaban is correct for non-APS unprovoked VTE; AVOID in triple-positive APS (EULAR APS 2019).
rationales.C: Aspirin alone is insufficient for secondary prevention after VTE in APS; full-intensity anticoagulation with warfarin is required. The anti-pattern is using antiplatelet for venous thrombosis. Aspirin is correct only for primary prevention in asymptomatic high-risk antiphospholipid antibody carriers (EULAR APS 2019).
rationales.D: Higher-intensity warfarin (INR 3-4) does NOT reduce thrombotic recurrence over standard intensity (INR 2-3) in venous APS (WAPS trial, RCT data), while increasing bleeding risk. Higher target is appropriate only for arterial APS or recurrent thrombosis despite INR 2-3 (EULAR APS 2019).
rationales.E: Unprovoked VTE with triple-positive APS carries a very high recurrence rate (annualised 7-10%); indefinite anticoagulation is recommended unless contraindicated. The anti-pattern is time-limited anticoagulation for high-recurrence thrombophilia. 3-month anticoagulation is correct only for clearly provoked VTE in low-risk patients (EULAR APS 2019).

rationales.A: Delaying steroid in a patient with visual symptoms risks irreversible blindness within hours; treat empirically and confirm with TAB after starting therapy (TAB remains positive for up to 2-4 weeks of steroid exposure). The anti-pattern is investigation-before-treatment in time-critical disease. Defer-steroid would be correct only without visual or ischaemic features and very low pretest probability (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.B: Low-dose steroid (15 mg) is the regimen for isolated polymyalgia rheumatica without GCA features. Visual symptoms require high-dose steroid (60-80 mg oral or IV pulse). The anti-pattern is conflating PMR and GCA dosing. Low-dose prednisolone is correct for isolated PMR with normal ophthalmic examination (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.D: Aspirin may be used adjunctively (reduces ischaemic complications) but does not replace steroid; alone it will not prevent visual loss. The anti-pattern is treating the vascular consequence without the underlying inflammation. Aspirin is correct as adjunct alongside high-dose steroid (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: MRI/MRA can support diagnosis (vessel-wall imaging) but does not replace TAB and must NOT delay sight-saving steroid. The anti-pattern is over-investigating in clinical emergencies. MRI is correct as adjunct after steroid commenced, not before (BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: Joint aspirate already shows crystals with negative Gram stain and the clinical picture fits gout; empirical antibiotics are not warranted while cultures pend. The anti-pattern is treating any monoarthritis as septic. Flucloxacillin is correct only with positive Gram stain, clinical sepsis features, or high pretest probability of septic arthritis (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.B: NSAIDs are relatively contraindicated with eGFR 38 and a prior duodenal ulcer; indomethacin in particular has high renal and GI risk. The anti-pattern is reflexive NSAID for gout without renal/GI assessment. NSAIDs are correct in young patients with normal renal function and no GI history (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).
rationales.C: Urate-lowering therapy is not started during an acute flare unless the patient is already established on it; initiating allopurinol acutely can prolong the flare by mobilising urate. The anti-pattern is treating the chronic problem during the acute crisis. Allopurinol is correct after flare settles (2-4 weeks) with concurrent prophylactic colchicine cover (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).
rationales.E: Old high-dose colchicine regimens (1 mg q1-2h to diarrhoea) are discarded because of toxicity (myelosuppression, neuropathy, multiorgan failure); current dosing caps at 1.5 mg in the first 24 hours, with renal dose-adjustment. Provoking diarrhoea is not a dosing endpoint - this is a classic legacy anti-pattern. High-dose colchicine is never correct (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).

rationales.A: KHA-CARI/Kidney Health Australia lists ACR persistently above 30 mg/mmol as an independent referral trigger, in addition to eGFR <30 or rapid decline. GP-only care at ACR >30 is below standard. The anti-pattern is anchoring on eGFR alone. GP-led care is correct for stable CKD G1-G3a with ACR <30 (KHA-CARI/Kidney Health Australia CKD Management in Primary Care 2024).
rationales.B: Waiting for eGFR to fall under 30 delays specialist input on renoprotection and dialysis planning. The anti-pattern is using a single referral trigger when KDIGO/KHA-CARI list multiple parallel triggers. eGFR-only referral threshold is correct only if ACR is consistently <30 mg/mmol (KHA-CARI/Kidney Health Australia CKD 2024).
rationales.C: SGLT2 inhibitors are continued for cardiorenal protection down to dialysis; empagliflozin is initiated at eGFR thresholds well below 45 for cardiorenal indications under PBS criteria. The anti-pattern is stopping renoprotective drugs because of stable eGFR. Cessation is correct only with acute haemodynamic deterioration, recurrent DKA, or symptomatic dehydration (AMH; PBS).
rationales.D: ACE inhibitors are renoprotective and should be continued unless creatinine rises >30% or potassium exceeds 6.0 mmol/L; his creatinine is stable and K is 4.6. The anti-pattern is reflexive cessation for any creatinine rise. Cessation is correct only with significant creatinine rise, hyperkalaemia >6.0, or symptomatic hypotension (Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular).

rationales.A: Australian and KDIGO guidance require correction of iron deficiency before starting an ESA. Functional iron deficiency (TSAT 14%) leads to inadequate ESA response and higher doses with associated cardiovascular risks. The anti-pattern is ESA-first without iron repletion. ESA is correct after iron stores are repleted and Hb remains <100 with symptoms (KDIGO Anaemia 2012/2024; KHA-CARI Anaemia 2023).
rationales.C: Oral iron is poorly absorbed and tolerated in CKD 4-5 due to elevated hepcidin (driven by inflammation and uraemia); IV iron is preferred. The anti-pattern is treating CKD anaemia like nutritional IDA. Oral iron is correct only in CKD 1-3 with minor functional deficiency, particularly if dialysis is not imminent (KHA-CARI Anaemia of CKD 2023).
rationales.D: Hb 96 g/L without bleeding or symptoms does not meet transfusion thresholds (typically <70-80 g/L). Transfusion sensitises potential transplant recipients via anti-HLA antibody development - a critical consideration in CKD progressing to transplant. Transfusion is correct only for symptomatic anaemia or bleeding (NBA Patient Blood Management Module 3).
rationales.E: Her pattern fits anaemia of CKD with functional iron deficiency; FOBT is negative and there are no GI symptoms. GI workup is reserved for unexplained IDA with red flags (weight loss, age >60, FOBT positive). The anti-pattern is endoscopy for any low ferritin regardless of context (Cancer Council Australia Colorectal Cancer Screening 2023).

rationales.A: Calcium-containing binders add to overall calcium load and are associated with greater progression of vascular calcification in CKD; KDIGO 2017 specifically recommends restricting their use, particularly when calcium is near upper limit. The anti-pattern is using calcium-based binders in modern CKD-MBD management. Calcium carbonate is correct only in hypocalcaemia or low-cost-constrained settings (KDIGO CKD-MBD 2017).
rationales.B: Activated vitamin D analogues (calcitriol, alfacalcidol) raise BOTH calcium and phosphate via gut absorption; starting calcitriol with phosphate 1.95 mmol/L will worsen hyperphosphataemia and accelerate vascular calcification. Phosphate control must come first. Calcitriol is correct only after phosphate is controlled and PTH remains elevated (KDIGO CKD-MBD 2017).
rationales.C: Cinacalcet (calcimimetic) is PBS-restricted to dialysis patients in Australia and lowers calcium; with corrected calcium already 2.18 hypocalcaemia is a risk. Cinacalcet is correct for dialysis-dependent secondary hyperparathyroidism with adequate calcium (PBS - cinacalcet; AMH).
rationales.E: Parathyroidectomy is reserved for severe medically refractory secondary hyperparathyroidism (PTH persistently >88 pmol/L despite optimal medical therapy) with hypercalcaemia/hyperphosphataemia or symptomatic disease. Medical therapy has not been escalated. Surgery is correct only after maximal medical management fails (KHA-CARI CKD-MBD).

rationales.A: Her bloods do not meet AEIOU criteria for emergency dialysis (no severe acidosis, refractory hyperkalaemia, fluid overload, uraemic encephalopathy/pericarditis, or toxic ingestion). Urgent dialysis bypasses stated preferences and provides no clear benefit in CFS 7 frailty. Dialysis is correct only with AEIOU criteria met and alignment with patient values (KHA-CARI Dialysis 2024).
rationales.C: Implementing PD without revisiting her stated preference 'not to live on a machine' is not patient-centred; PD requires significant manual dexterity and home support that CFS 7 status may not allow. The anti-pattern is offering modality without exploring values. PD is correct when aligned with patient values and capability (ANZSN Renal Supportive Care 2022).
rationales.D: Catheterisation does not address her uraemic symptoms or the need for a values-based conversation, and it adds CAUTI risk; her urine output is already documented. The anti-pattern is procedural action substituting for shared decision-making. IDC is correct only for urinary retention or accurate output monitoring in acute decompensation (ANZSN Renal Supportive Care 2022).
rationales.E: Hb 92 g/L without bleeding or haemodynamic compromise does not meet transfusion thresholds and risks fluid overload (already 3 L positive) and transplant sensitisation. Iron studies and ESA optimisation are standard. Transfusion is correct only for symptomatic anaemia, active bleeding, or Hb <70 in stable patients (NBA Patient Blood Management Module 3).

rationales.A: Orthostasis indicates volume depletion that explains the cause, but pre-renal physiology can progress to ATN if hypoperfusion is severe or prolonged. The discriminator between pre-renal and ATN is tubular function on urine indices, not the underlying haemodynamic state. Postural drop is supportive but not discriminatory (BMJ Best Practice AKI 2024).
rationales.B: A doubling of creatinine satisfies KDIGO stage 2 AKI but does not discriminate between pre-renal and ATN; both can produce the same magnitude of rise. The anti-pattern is using a severity marker as an aetiology marker. Creatinine doubling is correct for KDIGO staging only (KDIGO AKI 2012).
rationales.D: Hypernatraemia reflects free water deficit, supporting hypovolaemia, but it is not a discriminator between pre-renal AKI and ATN - both can occur in a dehydrated patient. The anti-pattern is using a volume-status marker as a tubular-function marker. Hypernatraemia is correct as evidence of dehydration, not as aetiology of AKI (BMJ Best Practice AKI 2024).
rationales.E: Absence of hydronephrosis rules out post-renal obstruction; it does not distinguish pre-renal from intrinsic causes. The anti-pattern is treating exclusion of one cause as confirmation of another. Negative ultrasound is correct for excluding obstruction, not as a positive discriminator between pre-renal and ATN (BMJ Best Practice AKI 2024).

rationales.A: ACR is well above the 0.5 g/day (UPCR ~50 mg/mmol) threshold at which KDIGO recommend initiating ACEi/ARB with BP target <120/70 in IgA nephropathy; observation alone is inadequate. The anti-pattern is watchful waiting in progressive proteinuric disease. Lifestyle-only is correct for proteinuria <0.5 g/day without other risk factors (KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).
rationales.B: Cyclophosphamide is reserved for rapidly progressive (crescentic) IgA nephropathy with crescents on biopsy and falling renal function. Not for stable disease with proteinuria around 1 g/day. The anti-pattern is escalating immunosuppression without histological indication. Cyclophosphamide is correct for biopsy-proven crescentic IgA (KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).
rationales.C: Systemic glucocorticoids are not first-line in IgA nephropathy; the TESTING and STOP-IgAN trials showed limited net benefit at general doses with significant toxicity. Steroids are considered only after maximal RAS blockade with persistent high-risk proteinuria, and increasingly replaced by targeted-release budesonide (Nefecon). Steroids are correct only after maximal RAS blockade fails (KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).
rationales.D: Tonsillectomy is used in some Japanese practice but is not supported by KDIGO or KHA-CARI as routine intervention outside that context; western RCT evidence is lacking. The anti-pattern is generalising regional practice. Tonsillectomy is correct in Japanese guidelines for recurrent tonsillitis-associated IgA, not standard in Australia (KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

rationales.A: Steroid monotherapy is inadequate for organ-threatening AAV with RPGN; combined induction with rituximab or cyclophosphamide is required to prevent ESKD. The anti-pattern is using only steroids in severe vasculitis. Steroid monotherapy is correct only in mild non-organ-threatening disease, never in RPGN (KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).
rationales.C: Plasma exchange is considered as adjunct in selected severe presentations (dialysis-requiring AAV, diffuse alveolar haemorrhage per PEXIVAS) but never as monotherapy; it must be combined with steroid + immunosuppressant. The anti-pattern is using TPE without backbone induction. TPE is correct as adjunct in DAH or rapid renal decline (KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021; PEXIVAS).
rationales.D: MMF has a role in remission MAINTENANCE and in selected non-severe AAV (MYCYC trial showed lower remission rates than cyclophosphamide), but not as induction monotherapy in severe organ-threatening disease. The anti-pattern is using maintenance regimen for induction. MMF is correct for maintenance after rituximab/cyclophosphamide induction (EULAR/ACR AAV 2022).
rationales.E: MPO-ANCA-positive RPGN with peripheral GGO is treated empirically once anti-GBM has been excluded; delay risks irreversible loss of nephrons. Biopsy is arranged but must not delay induction. The anti-pattern is biopsy-before-treatment in time-critical disease. Biopsy-first is correct only when diagnosis is uncertain without ANCA positivity (KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

rationales.A: Withholding treatment for TMA pending results is unsafe; untreated TTP carries >90% mortality. Empirical plasma exchange is standard of care when ADAMTS13 deficiency is clinically likely. The anti-pattern is investigation-before-treatment in a high-mortality disease. Await-results is correct only in stable patients with low pretest probability (ISTH TTP Guideline 2020).
rationales.B: Platelet transfusion in suspected TTP can precipitate further microvascular thrombosis ('feeding the fire') and is contraindicated unless life-threatening bleeding or essential procedure. The classic TTP anti-pattern is transfusing platelets to a number. Platelet transfusion is correct only with life-threatening bleeding or pre-procedure (ISTH TTP Guideline 2020).
rationales.C: Eculizumab is the treatment for atypical HUS (complement-mediated), suggested by predominant renal injury without severe ADAMTS13 deficiency. The clinical picture here is TTP-predominant. Eculizumab is correct only if ADAMTS13 is preserved and aHUS is diagnosed (KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).
rationales.E: DIC is unlikely with normal INR/APTT and normal fibrinogen; the picture is non-immune MAHA + thrombocytopenia with normal coagulation - the TMA signature, not DIC. The anti-pattern is treating any thrombocytopenia with bleeding as DIC. Empirical antibiotics are correct in suspected sepsis-DIC, not TMA (ISTH DIC vs TMA review).

rationales.A: Pre-renal AKI is supported by hypovolaemia, urine Na <20, concentrated urine; this patient is euvolaemic with sterile pyuria and eosinophiluria - not the pre-renal pattern. The anti-pattern is treating any AKI on ACEi as pre-renal. Pre-renal pattern is correct with documented volume depletion and low urine Na/FENa (KDIGO AKI 2012).
rationales.B: ATN typically follows a hypotensive, nephrotoxic, or ischaemic insult and shows muddy brown casts with urine Na >40 and FENa >2%; there is no haemodynamic insult here, and rash + eosinophilia + sterile pyuria fit AIN. The anti-pattern is missing the extra-renal clues to AIN. ATN is correct with appropriate insult history and muddy brown casts (KDIGO AKI 2012).
rationales.C: Pyelonephritis would show nitrites or bacteriuria, flank pain, and significant systemic features; sterile pyuria with eosinophiluria and no bacteria argues against UTI. Empirical aminoglycoside in AIN risks further nephrotoxicity. Pyelonephritis is correct with positive urinalysis and culture (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.D: RPGN presents with hypertension, oliguria, RED CELL CASTS, dysmorphic RBCs, and significant proteinuria. Sterile pyuria without red cell casts argues against a glomerular process. The anti-pattern is reaching for RPGN in any AKI with abnormal sediment. RPGN is correct with red cell casts + active sediment + nephritic features (KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

rationales.A: Forced diuresis with loop diuretics is not supported and may worsen volume status and AKI; the goal is to expand intravascular volume, not to force urine through depleted tubules. The anti-pattern is forcing output before adequate input. Frusemide is correct only after adequate fluid resuscitation if pulmonary oedema threatens (Therapeutic Guidelines: Nephrology).
rationales.B: Myoglobin is poorly cleared by haemodialysis (large molecular weight); dialysis is reserved for standard AKI indications (refractory K, severe acidosis, fluid overload, uraemia) rather than myoglobin removal per se. The anti-pattern is using dialysis to 'clear toxins' when fluid resuscitation is the primary therapy. Dialysis is correct for AEIOU indications, not for myoglobin (BMJ Best Practice - Rhabdomyolysis).
rationales.D: Routine mannitol is not supported by evidence and risks volume overload, hyperosmolarity, and AKI in its own right; isotonic crystalloid is the standard. The anti-pattern is legacy mannitol practice. Mannitol is correct only in selected ICU settings with intracranial hypertension, not rhabdomyolysis (BMJ Best Practice - Rhabdomyolysis).
rationales.E: Fluid restriction risks pigment cast nephropathy and irreversible AKI; intravascular volume is contracted after rhabdomyolysis even with normal BP, and aggressive crystalloid is needed. The anti-pattern is anchoring on BP alone for volume assessment. Restriction is essentially never correct in acute rhabdomyolysis (BMJ Best Practice - Rhabdomyolysis).

rationales.A: Peripheral IV potassium should not exceed 10 mmol/h (max 20 mmol/h in closely monitored area); 40 mmol/h risks chemical phlebitis, cardiac arrhythmia, and is unsafe peripherally. The anti-pattern is exceeding safe peripheral concentration. High-rate K is correct only via central access with continuous cardiac monitoring (AMH).
rationales.B: More oral potassium without addressing magnesium will not correct the deficit because urinary potassium losses continue while Mg is low (Mg keeps ROMK channels closed); large oral doses also risk GI side effects. The anti-pattern is treating the obvious electrolyte without checking cofactors. Oral K is correct as maintenance after acute correction (Therapeutic Guidelines: Electrolytes).
rationales.D: Long-term PPI use contributes to hypomagnesaemia and should be reviewed, but stopping it does not address the current life-threatening hypokalaemia with ECG changes; acute replacement is required now. The anti-pattern is treating the cause without addressing the consequence. PPI cessation is correct as longer-term measure (Therapeutic Guidelines: Electrolytes).
rationales.E: Bicarbonate would worsen the contraction alkalosis and drive K further intracellularly, deepening hypokalaemia. The anti-pattern is treating any acid-base abnormality reflexively without considering downstream effects on K. Bicarbonate is correct in severe acidosis (pH <7.1 or salicylate toxicity), not alkalosis (Therapeutic Guidelines: Electrolytes).

rationales.B: Isotonic saline is too slow to address acute cerebral oedema with seizures and may paradoxically WORSEN hyponatraemia in SIADH (de-saltation effect - kidneys excrete the Na while retaining free water). Hypertonic saline is the agent of choice for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia. Isotonic saline is correct in hypovolaemic hyponatraemia without severe symptoms (European hyponatraemia guidelines 2014).
rationales.C: Water restriction is appropriate for asymptomatic chronic SIADH but is inadequate for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia with seizure; rapid initial correction with hypertonic saline is needed first. The anti-pattern is using chronic-management strategy in acute neurological emergency. Fluid restriction is correct for stable SIADH without symptoms (European hyponatraemia guidelines 2014).
rationales.D: Vaptans risk over-rapid correction and ODS, especially in alcoholic malnourished patients; they are not recommended in severe symptomatic hyponatraemia at presentation. The anti-pattern is using a slow-onset oral agent in a seizure emergency. Tolvaptan is correct for stable euvolaemic SIADH after volume status confirmed (European hyponatraemia guidelines 2014).
rationales.E: Uncontrolled correction is the precise mechanism that produces ODS; an explicit cap on the 24-hour rise (8-10 mmol/L) is mandatory in high-risk patients. The anti-pattern is allowing spontaneous correction in a severe symptomatic patient. Spontaneous correction is acceptable only for asymptomatic chronic hyponatraemia after removing the cause (European hyponatraemia guidelines 2014).

rationales.A: Vitamin D toxicity raises BOTH calcium and phosphate (gut absorption of both) and shows a low PTH with HIGH 25-OH vitamin D; his 25-OH vitamin D is 62 nmol/L which is unremarkable. The anti-pattern is reaching for toxicity without supportive 25-OH value. Vitamin D toxicity is correct with 25-OH levels >250 nmol/L (BMJ Best Practice - Hypercalcaemia).
rationales.B: Primary hyperparathyroidism is commonest outpatient cause of hypercalcaemia but features an INAPPROPRIATELY NORMAL OR ELEVATED PTH; a PTH of 0.6 pmol/L (suppressed) rules it out. The anti-pattern is reflexive PHPT diagnosis without checking PTH. PHPT is correct with non-suppressed PTH plus hypercalcaemia (BMJ Best Practice - Hypercalcaemia).
rationales.D: Granulomatous hypercalcaemia is mediated by extra-renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase producing 1,25-OH vitamin D; calcium is typically modest, PTH is suppressed, and LYTIC LESIONS ARE NOT THE PICTURE. The anti-pattern is using one suppressed PTH to reach for granulomatous disease without other features. Sarcoid hypercalcaemia is correct with hilar lymphadenopathy and high 1,25-D (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: Thiazides modestly elevate calcium by enhanced distal tubular reabsorption and usually unmask underlying PHPT, with NON-SUPPRESSED PTH; severe hypercalcaemia and lytic lesions are not the pattern. Thiazide-induced is correct with mild hypercalcaemia and non-suppressed PTH on thiazide therapy (BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.B: Pure HAGMA would have delta ratio ~1 (anion gap rise = bicarbonate fall). Two days of vomiting predict delta ratio >1 because gastric H+ losses elevate bicarbonate relative to gap rise. Pure HAGMA is correct when delta ratio is between 1.0 and 2.0 with no other clinical evidence of co-existing acid-base process (BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base interpretation).
rationales.C: A delta ratio below 1 implies an additional non-anion-gap acidosis (diarrhoea, RTA, dilutional from saline); the calculated delta ratio here is above 1, not below 1. The anti-pattern is misreading delta-delta direction. Mixed HAGMA + NAGMA is correct with delta ratio <1, e.g., DKA plus saline-resuscitation acidosis (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.D: The primary disorder is metabolic (pH 7.06); the low pCO2 of 16 is compensatory hyperventilation (Kussmaul) for the acidosis, not a primary respiratory alkalosis. The anti-pattern is misclassifying compensation as primary disorder. Primary respiratory alkalosis is correct when pH >7.45 with primarily reduced pCO2 (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: The anion gap is 38, well above normal range (8-12), so this is a HAGMA not NAGMA. NAGMA + respiratory alkalosis is the wrong characterisation. The anti-pattern is misreading the anion gap. NAGMA + alkalosis is correct in salicylate toxicity (early), but here the gap is too high (BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: Hyperuricaemia is associated with pre-eclampsia and useful for prognosis but is NOT part of SOMANZ diagnostic criteria. The anti-pattern is conflating risk markers with diagnostic criteria. Hyperuricaemia is correct as a supportive prognostic feature in established PET, not for diagnosis (SOMANZ 2014).
rationales.B: Hyperreflexia is a clinical sign that raises suspicion of CNS involvement but is not a SOMANZ diagnostic criterion; the neurological criteria are eclampsia, persistent severe headache, visual scotomata, or stroke. Hyperreflexia would be correct as a magnesium-toxicity monitor, not as a PET diagnostic criterion (SOMANZ 2014).
rationales.D: Epigastric/RUQ pain raises suspicion for hepatic capsular stretch but is not a stand-alone SOMANZ diagnostic criterion without biochemical confirmation of hepatic dysfunction. RUQ pain plus transaminitis >2x ULN would be diagnostic; the anti-pattern is using symptoms without confirming biochemistry (SOMANZ 2014).
rationales.E: Significant proteinuria by SOMANZ requires PCR >=30 mg/mmol or 24h urine protein >=300 mg; 18 mg/mmol is below threshold. The anti-pattern is treating sub-threshold proteinuria as diagnostic. PCR >=30 mg/mmol would be diagnostic (SOMANZ 2014).

rationales.A: Load (2 g) and maintenance rate (0.5 g/h) are both subtherapeutic compared with SOMANZ regimen (4 g load + 1 g/h). The anti-pattern is under-dosing magnesium for fear of toxicity. Lower dose would be correct only with severe renal impairment (Cr >170 micromol/L) when 2 g/h replaces 1 g/h with serial level monitoring (SOMANZ 2014).
rationales.C: Maintenance rate is DOUBLE the recommended dose and reflex/respiratory monitoring is mandatory due to magnesium's narrow therapeutic window. The anti-pattern is over-dosing without monitoring. Higher dose would be correct only in confirmed therapeutic level falling below 1.7 mmol/L despite standard regimen, with intensive monitoring (SOMANZ 2014).
rationales.D: IM-only Pritchard regimen (10 g IM load + 5 g IM q4h) is used in low-resource settings without IV access; Australian practice uses IV which is more controllable, less painful, and avoids the gluteal abscess risk. IM regimen is correct only when IV is not feasible (SOMANZ 2014).
rationales.E: Loading dose should be given over 15-20 minutes, NOT 5 minutes, to avoid hypotension and bradycardia. Absent reflexes signal toxicity (level >3.5 mmol/L) and warrant calcium gluconate 1 g IV. Fast loading is correct only in established status eclampticus needing rapid effect, with concurrent BP and HR monitoring (SOMANZ 2014).

rationales.A: Maternal stabilisation with magnesium and BP control must precede delivery; rushing to theatre without stabilisation worsens both maternal and foetal outcomes. The anti-pattern is treating eclampsia as 'just deliver'. Immediate caesarean is correct only with confirmed foetal distress AFTER maternal stabilisation (SOMANZ 2014).
rationales.B: Magnesium sulphate is SUPERIOR to both diazepam and phenytoin for eclampsia (Magpie trial showed 58% reduction in recurrent seizures vs phenytoin) and is first-line. Benzodiazepines are reserved for refractory seizures after adequate magnesium loading. Diazepam-first is correct only when magnesium is unavailable or contraindicated (SOMANZ 2014).
rationales.C: Sublingual nifedipine causes precipitous, uncontrolled BP drops with placental hypoperfusion risk - a classic obstetric anti-pattern. Oral nifedipine SR or IV labetalol/hydralazine is preferred for controlled reduction. SL nifedipine is essentially never correct in pregnancy hypertension (SOMANZ 2014).
rationales.E: EEG is not required to diagnose or treat eclampsia; treatment is initiated on clinical grounds. The anti-pattern is over-investigating in clinical emergencies. EEG is correct only in post-ictal patients with persistent decreased GCS to exclude non-convulsive status, never before stabilising therapy (SOMANZ 2014).

rationales.B: AFLP typically causes HYPOGLYCAEMIA, COAGULOPATHY (raised INR, low fibrinogen), HYPERAMMONAEMIA, and more pronounced hyperbilirubinaemia. This patient has normal glucose, INR, fibrinogen, and ammonia. AFLP is correct when Swansea criteria are met (RCOG; BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.C: TTP is defined by ADAMTS13 activity <10%, severe thrombocytopenia, MAHA, neurological features, and fever; ADAMTS13 of 65% effectively excludes TTP. The anti-pattern is calling any MAHA + thrombocytopenia 'TTP'. TTP is correct only with ADAMTS13 <10% (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.D: HUS is dominated by acute kidney injury with relatively preserved transaminases; this patient has normal creatinine and very high transaminases - opposite pattern. The anti-pattern is conflating HUS and HELLP because both show MAHA + thrombocytopenia. HUS/aHUS is correct with renal-dominant injury (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: ICP causes pruritus (often palms/soles) and isolated raised bile acids with modest transaminitis WITHOUT thrombocytopenia or haemolysis. The anti-pattern is reaching for ICP based on transaminitis alone. ICP is correct with bile acids >19 micromol/L and pruritus, without HELLP features (RANZCOG ICP statement).

rationales.B: ADIPS criteria require only ONE elevated value to diagnose GDM (IADPSG single-value rule); her fasting 5.3 already meets the threshold. The anti-pattern is requiring multiple abnormal values (old WHO/two-step criteria). One-value rule is the Australian standard (ADIPS).
rationales.C: The Australian fasting cut-off is 5.1 mmol/L, not 5.5. The anti-pattern is using older/American thresholds in an Australian context. 5.5 mmol/L threshold was used historically but Australia adopted the IADPSG/ADIPS 5.1 in 2014 (ADIPS).
rationales.D: Diabetes outside pregnancy uses fasting >=7.0; in pregnancy the GDM cut-off is 5.1. She has GDM, not overt T2DM. Overt diabetes in pregnancy is diagnosed only with fasting >=7.0, HbA1c >=6.5%, or random glucose >=11.1 with symptoms - thresholds for T2DM diagnosis (ADIPS).
rationales.E: She meets diagnostic criteria; deferral risks foetal complications (macrosomia, stillbirth, neonatal hypoglycaemia). The anti-pattern is reassurance without acting on confirmed diagnostic results. Re-testing is correct only when initial OGTT is in borderline ambiguous range (very rare with the single-value rule) (ADIPS).

rationales.A: Full basal-bolus is reserved for patients with BOTH elevated fasting and postprandial values. This patient has isolated elevated fasting glucose only, so basal alone is sufficient. The anti-pattern is over-treating with multi-injection regimens. Basal-bolus is correct when both fasting and postprandial fail despite stepwise titration (ADIPS 2014; RANZCOG 2023).
rationales.B: Prandial insulin would not address the predominant fasting hyperglycaemia and would risk daytime hypoglycaemia. The anti-pattern is mismatching insulin profile to the glucose pattern. Prandial-only insulin is correct for isolated postprandial elevations with normal fasting (ADIPS 2014).
rationales.C: Fasting glucose remains above target despite four weeks of optimised lifestyle; pharmacotherapy is indicated to reduce risk of macrosomia and stillbirth. The anti-pattern is continued lifestyle-only when targets are not met. Continued lifestyle is correct only when targets are achieved (ADIPS 2014).
rationales.D: SGLT2 inhibitors are contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenicity concerns and lack of safety data (TGA category D). The anti-pattern is using non-pregnancy diabetes drugs in pregnancy. SGLT2i is never correct in current pregnancy management (TGA; AMH).

rationales.A: Labetalol is standard first-line in pregnancy hypertension, but in significant asthma (recent exacerbations on ICS-LABA) beta-blockade risks bronchospasm; SOMANZ and RANZCOG list significant asthma as a relative contraindication. Labetalol is correct in pregnancy hypertension without asthma or other beta-blocker contraindication (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: ACE inhibitors are CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy due to foetal renal failure, oligohydramnios, calvarial hypoplasia, and pulmonary hypoplasia. The classic absolute teratogen anti-pattern. Enalapril is never correct in current pregnancy; pre-conception switch to pregnancy-safe agent is mandatory (SOMANZ; AMH).
rationales.D: Methyldopa is an acceptable second-line agent but is less effective (higher target BP achievement failure) and has higher rates of depression/sedation/postural hypotension. Nifedipine is preferred in this asthma context. Methyldopa is correct as alternative second-line when first-line agents fail (SOMANZ).
rationales.E: Oral hydralazine causes reflex tachycardia and unpredictable response; IV hydralazine is used for severe acute hypertension but oral hydralazine is not first-line maintenance. The anti-pattern is using parenteral-only drugs orally. Oral hydralazine is correct only as add-on in resistant pregnancy hypertension with specialist input (SOMANZ).

rationales.B: Listeria is INTRINSICALLY RESISTANT to all cephalosporins, so ceftriaxone monotherapy is inadequate - a major anti-pattern. The intrinsic resistance is due to absence of penicillin-binding protein 3 affinity for cephalosporins. Ceftriaxone is correct in suspected gram-negative bacteraemia without Listeria suspicion (eTG).
rationales.C: Suspected listeriosis in pregnancy requires IV therapy until cultures and clinical improvement allow step-down; oral amoxicillin alone is insufficient given placental and CNS penetration concerns. Oral amoxicillin is correct as step-down after clinical and biochemical improvement on IV (eTG).
rationales.D: TMP-SMX is an alternative in penicillin-allergic patients but is contraindicated in pregnancy in T1 (folate antagonism, neural tube defects) and near term (kernicterus risk). The anti-pattern is using Bactrim-as-default in pregnancy. TMP-SMX is correct in penicillin-allergic non-pregnant patients (eTG).
rationales.E: Vancomycin is not first-line for Listeria; ampicillin + gentamicin is the recommended Australian regimen because of better bactericidal synergy. The anti-pattern is using vancomycin as default broad-spectrum. Vancomycin is correct in MRSA, severe penicillin-allergy with PJI/endocarditis, or selected severe gram-positive infection (eTG).

rationales.B: Pyrimethamine is a folate antagonist and is teratogenic in T1 (neural tube defects, oral clefts); it should be avoided before 14-18 weeks. Treatment of the foetus is reserved for confirmed foetal infection. The anti-pattern is treating mother as if foetus is already infected. Sulphadiazine-pyrimethamine-folinic acid is correct only after confirmed foetal infection on amniotic fluid PCR (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.C: Co-trimoxazole has activity against Toxoplasma but is not standard first-line therapy in pregnancy; spiramycin is preferred to prevent transmission and is the WHO-endorsed agent for prevention. Co-trimoxazole is correct in some non-pregnancy contexts (HIV-positive patients on prophylaxis) (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.D: Azithromycin has weak in vitro activity but is not a recommended first-line agent in pregnancy. The anti-pattern is using a familiar antibiotic without recognising it lacks the appropriate spectrum. Azithromycin is correct only as adjunct in some refractory toxoplasmic chorioretinitis regimens (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: Maternal primary T1 infection carries low vertical transmission risk (~10-15%) although severe consequences if transmitted; treatment with spiramycin substantially reduces transmission. Termination is not default. ToP is correct only after confirmed severe foetal infection (multiple anomalies on US/MRI) with informed maternal choice (RCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: Primary maternal CMV has SUBSTANTIALLY HIGHER vertical transmission rate (~30-40%) and higher severity than non-primary (~1-2%). The anti-pattern is misremembering the direction of risk. Non-primary higher-risk reassurance is never correct; the framing should always be 'primary infection = highest risk' (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.B: IV ganciclovir is teratogenic in animal studies and is not routinely used in pregnancy; valaciclovir trials have shown some benefit. The anti-pattern is using post-natal CMV treatment drug for maternal management. IV ganciclovir is correct for neonatal symptomatic congenital CMV, not maternal pregnancy (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.C: Spiramycin has NO role in CMV management; it is specific to toxoplasmosis. The anti-pattern is generalising one intrauterine infection drug to another. Spiramycin is correct only for maternal toxoplasmosis prevention of vertical transmission (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.D: Congenital CMV is the MOST COMMON congenital infection in developed countries (~0.5-1% of live births) and the leading non-genetic cause of sensorineural hearing loss. Serial foetal US plus amniotic PCR after 21 weeks is recommended. Reassurance without surveillance is never correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Oral prednisolone is safe in pregnancy and is essential for moderate/severe asthma exacerbation; withholding it risks maternal AND foetal hypoxia, which is FAR more harmful than transient corticosteroid exposure. The anti-pattern is treating pregnancy as steroid-contraindication. Steroid withholding is essentially never correct (Therapeutic Guidelines; National Asthma Council).
rationales.C: LTRAs (montelukast) are adjuncts, not first-line, and have NO role in acute exacerbation management. The anti-pattern is using maintenance-only drugs in acute exacerbations. Montelukast is correct as adjunct maintenance in exercise-induced or aspirin-sensitive asthma (National Asthma Council).
rationales.D: IV magnesium is reserved for severe exacerbations refractory to initial bronchodilator and corticosteroid therapy. The anti-pattern is leading with adjunctive therapy before standard care. IV magnesium is correct after 1 hour of maximal nebulised bronchodilator + systemic steroid with inadequate response (National Asthma Council).
rationales.E: PEFR 60% of best with a recent severe exacerbation does not meet safe discharge criteria; she needs systemic corticosteroids, observation, and a written asthma action plan. The anti-pattern is treating PEFR-alone as discharge criterion. Discharge is correct at PEFR >75% best with sustained response 1 hour after last salbutamol (National Asthma Council).

rationales.A: Valproate carries the highest major congenital malformation rate of all AEDs (~10%) plus 30-40% risk of neurodevelopmental impairment; contraindicated in women of childbearing potential unless all alternatives have failed. Folate dose for AED users is 5 mg, not 0.5 mg. Multiple errors. Continuing valproate is correct only after documented failure of all safer alternatives with informed consent (TGA; AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Phenytoin causes foetal hydantoin syndrome (microcephaly, cleft lip/palate, hypoplastic distal phalanges) with major malformation rate ~5-6%, higher than lamotrigine/levetiracetam. The anti-pattern is selecting older AEDs as 'safe'. Phenytoin is correct in some refractory epilepsy without safer alternatives, never as first-line pregnancy choice (Therapeutic Guidelines; AMH).
rationales.D: Polytherapy increases teratogenic risk in a dose-dependent fashion; valproate remains highly teratogenic at any dose. The goal is monotherapy with the safest agent. The anti-pattern is dose-reduction-instead-of-switch. Polytherapy is correct only in refractory epilepsy on a single safer agent, not adding lamotrigine to valproate (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Stopping AEDs risks status epilepticus, maternal injury, hypoxia, and foetal loss; uncontrolled seizures cause MORE foetal harm than well-chosen AEDs. The classic anti-pattern is 'safer to stop than treat'. Cessation is correct only after 2+ years seizure-free with neurology confirmation, NEVER reflexively in pregnancy (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Carbimazole in T1 risks teratogenicity (methimazole/carbimazole embryopathy: aplasia cutis, oesophageal/choanal atresia); positive TRAb crosses placenta with foetal goitre/tachycardia/hydrops risk requiring surveillance. Continuing carbimazole in T1 is the classic anti-pattern. Continuing carbimazole is correct in T2-T3 after T1 PTU switch (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Graves may improve in T2-T3 (immune modulation) but T1 has highest activity; abrupt cessation risks thyroid storm and miscarriage. The anti-pattern is assuming pregnancy 'cures' autoimmune disease. Stopping antithyroid drugs is correct only when biochemically euthyroid for >=6 months and TRAb negative (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Radioactive iodine is ABSOLUTELY contraindicated in pregnancy (foetal thyroid ablation, cretinism). The classic teratogen-by-mechanism anti-pattern. RAI is correct only post-partum if breastfeeding ceased and 6-month conception avoidance is acceptable (RANZCOG; AMH).
rationales.E: PTU causes severe hepatotoxicity (FDA black box); it should be limited to T1 and switched back to carbimazole in T2-T3. The anti-pattern is assuming one regimen suits all trimesters. PTU throughout is correct only in carbimazole hypersensitivity or thyroid storm (RANZCOG; AMH).

rationales.A: Dose typically needs to INCREASE by 25-30% in early pregnancy due to increased thyroid hormone requirement; TSH should be checked every 4 weeks until mid-pregnancy. Trimester-specific upper TSH targets are LOWER than 4.0 (typically <2.5 T1, <3.0 T2-T3). Multiple errors. This regimen is correct only outside pregnancy (Endocrine Society; ATA).
rationales.B: Although beta-hCG can transiently suppress TSH in T1, women with established hypothyroidism need a DOSE INCREASE of ~25-30%, not reduction, due to higher TBG and placental transfer. The anti-pattern is overgeneralising physiological T1 TSH suppression to hypothyroid women on replacement. Dose reduction is essentially never correct in pregnancy (Endocrine Society).
rationales.C: Stopping levothyroxine risks severe hypothyroidism, miscarriage, and impaired neurodevelopment; continue and increase dose. The anti-pattern is 'wash-out' assessment of endogenous function during pregnancy. Stopping levothyroxine is correct only with documented full thyroid recovery (e.g., transient post-partum thyroiditis resolved) (Endocrine Society).
rationales.E: Symptoms are unreliable in pregnancy (overlap with normal pregnancy symptoms - fatigue, weight gain, constipation); routine 4-weekly TSH monitoring until mid-pregnancy is required because of dynamic dose requirements. The anti-pattern is symptom-guided monitoring in pregnancy. Symptom monitoring alone is correct only in stable euthyroid post-partum patients (Endocrine Society).

rationales.A: Risk of 1 in 80 is well above the high-risk threshold (commonly 1 in 300 in Australian labs) and NT >=3.0 mm independently warrants further evaluation. The anti-pattern is treating any individual risk as 'small enough to ignore'. Low-risk reassurance is correct only with cFTS risk <1:1000 or NIPT low-probability result (RANZCOG Prenatal screening).
rationales.B: cFTS is a single screening event; repeating it will not move risk meaningfully and delays definitive evaluation. The anti-pattern is using screening tests as serial monitoring tools. Repeat cFTS is essentially never correct as a follow-up of high risk (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Maternal age alone is no longer an indication for invasive diagnostic testing; risk is individualised by cFTS or NIPT. The anti-pattern is using maternal age as standalone trigger when modern combined screens are available. CVS-by-age was the pre-2000 practice and is now superseded (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Amniocentesis is not performed before 15 completed weeks because of higher procedure-related foetal loss and limb defect risk (Canadian Early Amniocentesis Trial showed talipes equinovarus excess at 11-12 weeks). CVS is the T1 diagnostic option. Amniocentesis is correct from 15 weeks (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Even at advanced maternal age, NIPT is a SCREENING test not diagnostic. False positives occur (confined placental mosaicism, vanishing twin, maternal mosaicism, maternal malignancy). Irreversible decisions should not be made on screening alone. The anti-pattern is treating high PPV as 100% PPV (RANZCOG; HGSA).
rationales.B: Amniocentesis before 15 completed weeks is associated with higher foetal loss and talipes (early amniocentesis); CVS is the correct first-trimester diagnostic test. Amniocentesis is correct after 15 weeks if she prefers to wait, with the trade-off of later decisional timing (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Repeating NIPT does not resolve the screening-diagnostic distinction; the same biological mechanism producing the positive (e.g., CPM) will produce the same result. Diagnostic testing (karyotype on tissue) is required. Repeat NIPT is correct only with technical failure (low fetal fraction), not for confirming positive results (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Normal NT and morphology scan reduce but do not exclude T21; up to 50% of T21 foetuses have normal NT and ultrasound is not diagnostic. The anti-pattern is using sonographic 'normality' to rule out aneuploidy. Reassurance from normal scan is correct only with low-risk screening and no flagged findings (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Transcervical CVS has slightly higher infection and miscarriage rates than transabdominal in most series and is used when placenta is posterior and inaccessible transabdominally. 2-3% overstates the risk in experienced units; modern figure is 0.1-0.2%. The anti-pattern is using old textbook risk figures (RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Amniocentesis before 15 weeks (early amniocentesis) is no longer offered because of higher foetal loss and talipes equinovarus per the Canadian Early Amniocentesis Trial. CVS is the correct T1 option. Early amniocentesis is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: NIPT is non-invasive but remains a SCREENING test; she has already had high-risk cFTS and explicitly wants a definitive answer. Offering only NIPT does not meet her stated need. NIPT is correct as contingent screen if she preferred to defer diagnostic testing (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Morphology scan is a screening tool, not diagnostic for aneuploidy, and does not satisfy her wish for early definitive information; up to 50% of T21 foetuses have a normal morphology scan. Deferring to morphology-only would be correct only if she explicitly declined diagnostic testing (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: NIPT can be offered if the woman wishes but is not indicated by isolated EIF in a woman with low-risk cFTS; residual probability of aneuploidy is too low for routine contingent screening to be cost-effective. NIPT is correct in higher-risk soft marker combinations or maternal request, not routine isolated EIF (RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Isolated EIF in a low-risk population carries a small likelihood ratio (1.4-1.8) that does not meaningfully change a prior risk of 1:4200; offering amniocentesis exposes the woman to procedure-related miscarriage risk EXCEEDING the residual aneuploidy probability. The anti-pattern is offering invasive testing for low-yield soft markers (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Isolated EIF without other findings on high-quality four-chamber, outflow tract, and three-vessel views is not an indication for foetal echocardiography. Foetal echo is correct for diabetes, NT >=3.5 mm, family history of CHD, or other suspicious findings (RANZCOG; ASUM).
rationales.D: Isolated EIF is a static finding requiring no surveillance; repeat scanning is not recommended unless there are growth or other concerns. The anti-pattern is serial scanning for benign findings, which generates anxiety without yield. Repeat scan is correct only with growth concerns or new symptoms (ASUM).

rationales.A: Australian NBA guideline states anti-D is not routinely indicated for a sensitising event before 12 completed weeks, EXCEPT for CVS, ectopic pregnancy, molar pregnancy, or termination of pregnancy. The anti-pattern is reflexive anti-D for any bleed regardless of gestation. Anti-D in T1 is correct for the specific exceptions only (NBA Anti-D guidelines).
rationales.B: 1500 IU is the UK single-dose antenatal regimen at 28 weeks; Australia uses TWO-dose 625 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks, not single 1500 IU. The anti-pattern is conflating UK and Australian regimens. 1500 IU single dose is correct only in UK practice (NBA).
rationales.C: 250 IU is the UK dose; the Australian dose for first-trimester sensitising events (when indicated, e.g., after CVS) is 250 IU pre-12 weeks per NBA. Kleihauer testing is recommended for events from 20 weeks, not first-trimester bleeding. The anti-pattern is using Kleihauer in T1 (NBA).
rationales.E: Anti-D is given for sensitising events when they occur, not prophylactically deferred; routine antenatal prophylaxis begins at 28 weeks, not 12. The anti-pattern is conflating event-based dosing with routine prophylaxis schedules. Deferral is essentially never correct in anti-D management (NBA).

rationales.A: Benzylpenicillin is first-line in non-allergic women but is contraindicated by her documented immediate hypersensitivity reaction (anaphylaxis-spectrum). The anti-pattern is overlooking documented severe allergy. Benzylpenicillin is correct in non-allergic GBS-positive women (RANZCOG GBS; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.B: Cefazolin is the alternative in women with LOW-RISK penicillin allergy (non-anaphylactic, non-immediate). Her immediate hypersensitivity reaction with mucosal involvement is high-risk and cephalosporins are not recommended due to cross-reactivity risk. Cefazolin is correct only in low-risk allergy (rash without mucosal/anaphylaxis features) (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.D: Clindamycin is an option in high-risk penicillin allergy only when GBS sensitivity to clindamycin is CONFIRMED on testing; her isolate is resistant. The anti-pattern is using clindamycin without sensitivity testing (which is required for GBS isolates). Clindamycin is correct with documented sensitivity (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.E: Oral therapy does not achieve adequate cord blood levels for intrapartum prophylaxis, and her GBS isolate is resistant to erythromycin. The anti-pattern is using oral antibiotics in labour. Oral erythromycin has no role in intrapartum GBS prophylaxis (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

rationales.B: WHO 7.0/11.1 thresholds are for OVERT diabetes diagnosis outside pregnancy; Australian ADIPS thresholds for GDM are 5.1/10.0/8.5. The anti-pattern is applying non-pregnancy thresholds to pregnancy. WHO 7.0 cut-off is correct for diagnosing overt diabetes in early pregnancy (a separate diagnostic category from GDM) (ADIPS).
rationales.C: ADIPS adopts the IADPSG single-value rule: any single value at or above threshold is diagnostic; two abnormal values are NOT required. The anti-pattern is requiring multiple abnormalities (old WHO/Carpenter-Coustan two-step criteria). One-value rule is the Australian standard (ADIPS).
rationales.D: 5.3 mmol/L exceeds the ADIPS fasting threshold of 5.1; meeting ANY ONE threshold is diagnostic regardless of the other two values. The anti-pattern is focusing on the most-abnormal-looking value rather than applying threshold rules. All three values >= thresholds are not required (ADIPS).
rationales.E: HbA1c is not used as the primary diagnostic test for GDM (lacks sensitivity in T2 due to increased red cell turnover); OGTT remains the standard. HbA1c is correct for early-pregnancy screening for overt T2DM (>=6.5%), not as GDM diagnostic test (ADIPS).

rationales.A: Threatened miscarriage requires confirmation of foetal VIABILITY; the foetal pole has CRL 12 mm with no cardiac activity, diagnostic of foetal demise. The anti-pattern is calling any bleeding-with-closed-os 'threatened' without checking viability. Threatened is correct with confirmed viable IUP plus bleeding plus closed os (RANZCOG; ASUM).
rationales.C: Inevitable miscarriage requires an OPEN cervical os; her os is closed. The anti-pattern is conflating clinical labels. Inevitable is correct with open os, viable or non-viable pregnancy retained, often with bleeding (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Incomplete miscarriage requires that pregnancy tissue has been partially passed, with retained products on US; she has had no significant bleeding and the pregnancy tissue is intact. The anti-pattern is over-using 'incomplete' label. Incomplete is correct after partial expulsion with retained POC (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Complete miscarriage requires an empty uterus on US following passage of all products; her uterus contains the foetal pole and gestational sac. The anti-pattern is calling tissue-retained scenarios 'complete'. Complete miscarriage is correct with empty uterus and resolving symptoms (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: RANZCOG/RCOG eligibility criteria include adnexal mass <35 mm and absence of foetal cardiac activity; her mass is within range and there is no cardiac activity. This finding does NOT contraindicate methotrexate. Adnexal mass >=35 mm WOULD contraindicate methotrexate per most protocols (RANZCOG; RCOG).
rationales.B: Methotrexate eligibility usually requires beta-hCG <5000 IU/L (some units 1500-5000); 3800 is below threshold. The anti-pattern is calling sub-threshold values as contraindicating. hCG >5000 WOULD contraindicate single-dose MTX in favour of multi-dose or surgical management (RANZCOG; RCOG).
rationales.D: Mild pain in a stable patient with no peritonism is not a contraindication and is common in tubal ectopic; only acute severe pain or peritonism would mandate surgery. The anti-pattern is treating any tenderness as 'unstable'. Severe abdominal pain with peritonism WOULD contraindicate MTX (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Good access to care and willingness to follow up are PRE-REQUISITES for outpatient methotrexate, supporting rather than contraindicating this option. Long distance, unreliable transport, or inability to attend regular follow-up WOULD contraindicate MTX in favour of inpatient or surgical management (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: PUL with hCG below the transvaginal discriminatory zone (1500-2000 IU/L) and no ectopic seen on US is NOT YET diagnosed as ectopic; treating with MTX could terminate a viable early IUP. The classic anti-pattern is presumptive treatment in PUL. MTX is correct only with confirmed ectopic or after IUP confidently excluded with rising/plateauing hCG above discriminatory zone (RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Diagnostic laparoscopy is reserved for haemodynamic instability, suspected rupture, or persistent diagnostic uncertainty after failed expectant management. The anti-pattern is reflexive surgery in stable PUL. Laparoscopy is correct in haemodynamic instability or after failed conservative management (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: A high-quality scan has already been performed; immediate repeat scanning is low-yield. Waiting for hCG to cross the discriminatory zone is the standard approach. Repeat scan is correct only when there is concern about initial scan quality or new symptoms (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Reassurance without exclusion of ectopic risks rupture; PUL must be followed up to a definitive diagnosis (IUP, failing PUL, or ectopic). The anti-pattern is premature closure on 'normal pregnancy' diagnosis. Reassurance is correct only after IUP confirmed on follow-up scan (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Severe PUQE, postural hypotension, hypokalaemia, ketonuria, and >5% weight loss are explicit admission indications. Outpatient antiemetics will fail. The anti-pattern is under-treating hyperemesis as 'morning sickness'. Outpatient management is correct in mild-moderate hyperemesis without dehydration or weight loss (RANZCOG; SOMANZ Hyperemesis).
rationales.C: TPN is reserved for women who fail standard rehydration and antiemetic regimens with persistent weight loss; it carries significant infection/thromboembolism risk (PICC complications). The anti-pattern is escalating to TPN before exhausting stepwise antiemetics. TPN is correct only after failure of standard escalation (RANZCOG; SOMANZ).
rationales.D: Dextrose-first rehydration without thiamine cover risks WERNICKE'S ENCEPHALOPATHY in hyperemesis - a critical preventable iatrogenic injury. Use saline; give thiamine BEFORE any dextrose. The classic 'glucose before thiamine' anti-pattern is essentially never correct in malnourished/hyperemesis patients (RANZCOG; SOMANZ).
rationales.E: Termination is rarely indicated and only after exhausting medical management with the woman's informed wish; the great majority of hyperemesis resolves by 16-20 weeks. The anti-pattern is offering ToP without trial of escalating therapy. ToP is correct only after refractory disease with maternal informed decision (RANZCOG; SOMANZ).

rationales.A: Methotrexate is first-line for persistent gestational trophoblastic NEOPLASIA, not for primary evacuation of a hydatidiform mole. Surgical evacuation is the primary treatment. The anti-pattern is using GTN treatment as primary mole management. MTX is correct for persistent GTN after evacuation with rising hCG (RANZCOG GTD).
rationales.C: Molar pregnancies do not reliably resolve spontaneously; surgical evacuation with histological confirmation is required to confirm diagnosis and remove trophoblastic tissue. The anti-pattern is expectant management of confirmed mole. Expectant management is correct only for very small failed early pregnancies, not molar (RANZCOG GTD).
rationales.D: Hysterectomy is reserved for women who have completed their family AND have a complete mole, or for refractory disease; suction curettage is standard first-line for women wishing to preserve fertility. Hysterectomy is correct in completed family with high-risk complete mole or persistent disease (RANZCOG GTD).
rationales.E: Medical evacuation with uterotonics/prostaglandins is associated with HIGHER rates of incomplete evacuation, embolisation of trophoblastic tissue, and persistent GTN. Suction curettage is preferred. The anti-pattern is treating molar pregnancy like missed miscarriage (RANZCOG GTD).

rationales.B: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy is treated regardless of dipstick (5-10% progress to pyelonephritis if untreated). GBS bacteriuria additionally mandates intrapartum prophylaxis. The anti-pattern is using dipstick negativity to defer treatment. No treatment is correct only outside pregnancy with no symptoms (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: GBS bacteriuria is an INDEPENDENT indication for intrapartum GBS prophylaxis at delivery; antenatal treatment does not eliminate this need (the rectovaginal colonisation persists). The anti-pattern is confusing antenatal treatment with intrapartum prophylaxis - they are distinct interventions (RANZCOG GBS).
rationales.D: Pregnant ASB warrants prompt empirical treatment, not deferral; growth at 10^8 CFU/L is diagnostic. The anti-pattern is repeat testing for confirmation in a clear-cut diagnosis. Repeat MSU is correct only for borderline results <10^5 CFU/mL or contamination concern (eTG).
rationales.E: Continuous suppression is reserved for recurrent UTI in pregnancy after multiple confirmed episodes, not single ASB. Nitrofurantoin is also avoided near term (haemolysis risk). Continuous suppression is correct after >=2 confirmed UTIs in pregnancy (eTG).

rationales.A: Methotrexate is CONTRAINDICATED when a viable intrauterine pregnancy coexists with an ectopic (heterotopic) because it would terminate the IUP. The critical anti-pattern is treating heterotopic like isolated ectopic. MTX is correct only for isolated ectopic without coexistent IUP (RANZCOG; RCOG).
rationales.B: Heterotopic with 30 mm adnexal mass, increasing pain, dizziness, and free fluid carries high rupture risk; expectant management is not appropriate. The beta-hCG trajectory is uninterpretable because the IUP confounds it. Expectant management is correct only for small ectopic with falling hCG without IUP (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Watchful waiting in a haemodynamically stable but symptomatic heterotopic with adnexal mass and free fluid is unsafe; rupture risk is high. The anti-pattern is reactive rather than planned surgery. Watchful waiting is correct only in asymptomatic small heterotopic with no rupture features (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: KCl injection is used in interstitial or cornual ectopics where surgical access is hazardous, but not first-line for a tubal heterotopic with rupture risk and prior salpingectomy. The anti-pattern is matching invasive technique to inappropriate setting. KCl is correct in interstitial/cornual ectopics or cervical ectopics where surgery would cause major haemorrhage (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Asking in the presence of the suspected perpetrator places the woman at increased risk of escalation and produces unreliable answers; the classic IPV-screening anti-pattern. Partner-present enquiry is essentially never correct in IPV screening (RACGP IPV; WHO).
rationales.C: Antenatal enquiry is recommended specifically because pregnancy is a high-risk period for IPV escalation; deferring to postnatal misses the engagement window. Postnatal-only enquiry is correct only when antenatal opportunity is missed (RACGP).
rationales.D: Visible leaflets in shared records are unsafe if the partner reviews them; safe enquiry in private is recommended. Written information is correct only when given discreetly during private consultation, not via shared documents (RACGP).
rationales.E: Mandatory police reporting against the woman's wishes endangers her and damages the therapeutic alliance; she must consent to any external action. Police involvement is correct only with the woman's consent or where mandatory child-protection thresholds apply (RACGP; WHO).

rationales.B: Bolting on an AHW as a separate referral is less effective than an integrated model where AHW and midwife share continuity from start; the anti-pattern is treating cultural safety as 'add-on'. Standard midwifery with AHW referral is correct only when AMIHS is unavailable (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Generalising all Aboriginal pregnancies as high-risk is racist and contradicts evidence; risk should be individually assessed. Obstetrician-led care is correct based on individual clinical risk factors, not ethnicity (RANZCOG; AHMAC).
rationales.D: GP shared care is a valid model but does not provide the integrated cultural and clinical continuity that AMIHS offers. GP shared care is correct in low-risk pregnancies where cultural safety is met by other means (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Telehealth is an adjunct, not a substitute for face-to-face culturally safe care; removing in-person AHW/midwife contact reduces engagement. Telehealth is correct as supplement to face-to-face care, not replacement (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Oral prophylaxis has higher failure rates due to adherence and absorption; IM BPG is preferred. Oral PenV is correct only when IM cannot be tolerated and adherence is supervised (RHDAustralia Guideline).
rationales.C: Penicillin is safe across all trimesters; cessation risks ARF recurrence with permanent valve worsening. Cessation is essentially never correct in active RHD secondary prophylaxis (RHDAustralia Guideline; AMH).
rationales.D: Standard 4-weekly interval is retained for routine prophylaxis; 3-weekly is reserved for documented breakthrough ARF or selected high-risk situations per specialist advice, not pregnancy alone. 3-weekly is correct after documented breakthrough recurrence (RHDAustralia Guideline).
rationales.E: Macrolides are only used for documented penicillin allergy; pregnancy does not cause allergy. Azithromycin monthly is correct only in confirmed penicillin allergy (RHDAustralia Guideline).

rationales.B: Beta-agonist tocolysis causes tachycardia and pulmonary oedema in severe MS - contraindicated. Valvuloplasty is considered antenatally in refractory disease, not in established labour at term. Tocolysis is correct in preterm labour without severe MS (RHDAustralia; ESC 2018).
rationales.C: Foetus is reassuring and labour is progressing; GA carries high haemodynamic risk in severe MS. Vaginal birth with assisted second stage under regional anaesthesia is preferred. Emergency CS is correct with foetal distress or maternal cardiovascular collapse (RANZCOG W-WH 22; ESC 2018).
rationales.D: Fluid bolus in pulmonary congestion from severe MS worsens pulmonary oedema; preload must be preserved but not increased. Fluid bolus is correct only in clear hypovolaemia, never with pulmonary oedema (RHDAustralia; ESC 2018).
rationales.E: ECG shows sinus tachycardia, not AF; amiodarone is reserved for arrhythmia and carries foetal thyroid risk. Amiodarone is correct only in confirmed maternal arrhythmia refractory to safer agents (RHDAustralia; AMH).

rationales.A: Victorian Act permits TOP on request up to 24 weeks; this is correct on the law, but BEST practice for advanced gestation with foetal anomaly is the two-doctor tertiary pathway (option C). Option A understates the appropriate referral pathway. On-request alone is correct for early gestations without anomaly (Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).
rationales.B: Victoria has no 20-week prohibition; TOP is on-request to 24 weeks and lawful after 24 weeks with two doctors. The anti-pattern is misremembering interstate variations. The 20-week limit applies in some other states (Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).
rationales.D: Victoria does not require ethics committee approval; the framework is the Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 with practitioner-led decision making. Ethics committee approval was the pre-2008 NSW practice (Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).
rationales.E: No statutory list of qualifying anomalies; the test for TOP after 24 weeks is two-doctor reasonable belief considering all relevant circumstances. A statutory-list framework would be correct only in jurisdictions with such legislation (none in Australia) (Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).

rationales.B: 600 mg mifepristone is the older regimen replaced because 200 mg is equally effective. 400 microg oral misoprostol has lower completion rates than 800 microg buccal/sublingual/vaginal. Higher mifepristone dose was the original Roussel-Uclaf regimen, now obsolete (MS-2 Step PI; RANZCOG TOP).
rationales.C: Methotrexate-misoprostol is used for medical management of ectopic pregnancy and historically for very early TOP, but is not the registered Australian protocol; MS-2 Step is standard. MTX-miso is correct for ectopic pregnancy management (MS-2 Step PI; RANZCOG TOP).
rationales.D: Misoprostol-only regimens have lower success (~85%) than mifepristone-misoprostol (~95-98%); used only when mifepristone is unavailable. Miso-alone is correct in resource-limited settings without mifepristone access (RANZCOG TOP; Family Planning Australia).
rationales.E: Simultaneous administration has lower efficacy than the 36-48 hour interval; the gap is required for mifepristone-induced cervical priming and progesterone receptor antagonism. Same-visit administration is correct only in selected emergency contexts where 36-48 hours wait is impossible (MS-2 Step PI; RANZCOG TOP).
